title: DORA ICT Access Control and Authentication Monitoring

compliance.assurance_rationale: DORA Art.9(4)(c) — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that DORA Art.9(4)(c) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.3.15: DORA ICT Access Control and Authentication Monitoring.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.3.15' running on CIM Authentication data model, index=auth, index=windows, index=azure, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110)
spl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count dc(Authentication.src) as src_count dc(Authentication.dest) as dest_count
  from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication
  where Authentication.action="success"
  by Authentication.user Authentication.app span=1d
| rename Authentication.* as *
| lookup dora_critical_systems.csv system_name AS dest OUTPUT critical_function
| where isnotnull(critical_function)
| eval risk_signals=mvappend(
    if(src_count > 5, "MULTI_LOCATION", null()),
    if(match(lower(user),"(?i)shared|generic|test|admin[0-9]"), "SHARED_ACCOUNT", null()),
    if(match(lower(app),"(?i)password|basic|ntlm") AND NOT match(lower(app),"(?i)mfa|2fa|cert|kerberos"), "WEAK_AUTH", null()))
| where isnotnull(risk_signals)
| table user, app, critical_function, count, src_count, dest_count, risk_signals
| sort - count
description: Article 9(4)(c) requires policies for digital identity management, access control limiting physical and logical access to ICT assets and data, and strong authentication mechanisms. This use case monitors authentication patterns across DORA-regulated ICT systems — detecting shared accounts, weak authentication, privilege escalation, and access from unauthorized locations — providing the access control evidence DORA mandates for protection and prevention.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for DORA compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes auth, azure, windows. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Ensure CIM Authentication data model is populated from domain controllers, IdPs, and cloud auth sources; (2) tag critical ICT systems in `dora_critical_systems.csv`; (3) alert on shared accounts accessing critical functions; (4) detect authentication without MFA for privileged operations; (5) report access control compliance to management body; (6) correlate with HR data for joiner/mover/leaver validation. Indexes required: `index=auth`, `index=azure`, `index=windows`.
visualization: Table (access control risks), Bar chart (risks by type), Single value (shared account usage), Heatmap (user × critical system access).
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src | sort - count

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services

knownFalsePositives: Incidents during planned ICT changes, controlled DR drills, or after major upgrade waves.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: CIM Authentication data model, `index=auth`, `index=windows`, `index=azure`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Ensure CIM Authentication data model is populated from domain controllers, IdPs, and cloud auth sources; (2) tag critical ICT systems in `dora_critical_systems.csv`; (3) alert on shared accounts accessing critical functions; (4) detect authentication without MFA for privileged operations; (5) report access control compliance to management body; (6) correlate with HR data for joiner/mover/leaver validation.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` count dc(Authentication.src) as src_count dc(Authentication.dest) as dest_count
  from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication
  where Authentication.action="success"
  by Authentication.user Authentication.app span=1d
| rename Authentication.* as *
| lookup dora_critical_systems.csv system_name AS dest OUTPUT critical_function
| where isnotnull(critical_function)
| eval risk_signals=mvappend(
    if(src_count > 5, "MULTI_LOCATION", null()),
    if(match(lower(user),"(?i)shared|generic|test|admin[0-9]"), "SHARED_ACCOUNT", null()),
    if(match(lower(app),"(?i)password|basic|ntlm") AND NOT match(lower(app),"(?i)mfa|2fa|cert|kerberos"), "WEAK_AUTH", null()))
| where isnotnull(risk_signals)
| table user, app, critical_function, count, src_count, dest_count, risk_signals
| sort - count
```

Understanding this SPL

**DORA ICT Access Control and Authentication Monitoring (Art. 9(4)(c))** — Article 9(4)(c) requires policies for digital identity management, access control limiting physical and logical access to ICT assets and data, and strong authentication mechanisms. This use case monitors authentication patterns across DORA-regulated ICT systems — detecting shared accounts, weak authentication, privilege escalation, and access from unauthorized locations — providing the access control evidence DORA mandates for protection and prevention.

Documented **Data sources**: CIM Authentication data model, `index=auth`, `index=windows`, `index=azure`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Authentication.Authentication` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Renames fields with `rename` for clarity or joins.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where isnotnull(critical_function)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **risk_signals** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• Filters the current rows with `where isnotnull(risk_signals)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

See also the **CIM SPL** field in this use case; enable the matching data model acceleration if you run the tstats variant in production.

Output fields: app, count, critical_function, dest_count, risk_signals, src_count, user.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare with audit evidence in the source system (DLP, IDP, or application) and, if this use case underpins a control test, the ServiceNow GRC console or the evidence pack for a known time window.


Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (access control risks), Bar chart (risks by type), Single value (shared account usage), Heatmap (user × critical system access).
grandmaExplanation: We stay on top of ict access control and authentication monitoring — so we can show we govern technology and resilience risk the way financial rules that apply to us require.
